skill.name: layout
skill.description: Improve layout, spacing, and visual rhythm. Fixes monotonous grids, inconsistent spacing, and weak visual hierarchy. Use when the user mentions layout feeling off, spacing issues, visual hierarchy, crowded UI, alignment problems, or wanting better composition.
skill.summary: Transform monotonous, crowded interfaces into intentional layouts with systematic spacing, visual rhythm, and clear hierarchy.
skill.author: pbakaus

security_audit.summary: Static scanner flagged 27 patterns but all are false positives. The detected 'external_commands' and 'weak cryptographic' patterns are CSS syntax references (custom properties like --space-xs, grid-template-areas) and design terminology (spacing scales, visual hierarchy). No executable code, network requests, or data exfiltration found. This is a documentation-only skill.

low_findings.title: Static Scanner False Positive - CSS Syntax Misinterpreted
low_findings.description: The static scanner incorrectly flagged CSS property references (--space-xs, gap, grid-template-areas) as Ruby/shell backtick execution. These are documentation examples of CSS syntax for layout improvements, not executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct inspection confirms these are CSS property names and code examples in documentation, not executable commands. The skill contains no code execution patterns.

low_findings.title: Static Scanner False Positive - Design Terminology Flagged
low_findings.description: The static scanner flagged design terms like 'system', 'scale', 'grid' as security risks. In context, these refer to spacing systems, visual design scales, and CSS grid layouts - legitimate UI/UX concepts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Contextual analysis confirms all flagged terms are standard design documentation. No system reconnaissance, cryptographic operations, or security-sensitive code present.

content.user_title: Create Polished Layouts with Systematic Spacing
content.value_statement: Fix monotonous grids and inconsistent spacing that make interfaces feel amateurish. This skill establishes systematic spacing scales, visual rhythm, and clear hierarchy to transform generic layouts into intentional compositions.
actual_capabilities: Analyze layouts for spacing inconsistencies, weak hierarchy, and monotonous repetition
Establish systematic spacing scales using framework tokens or custom CSS variables
Apply visual rhythm principles through tight grouping and generous separation
Select appropriate layout tools (Flexbox for 1D, Grid for 2D) for specific use cases
Break card grid monotony through varied sizing, asymmetric compositions, and mixed content types
Implement semantic z-index and shadow scales for consistent depth management
limitations: Requires user to provide existing code or design context - cannot create layouts from scratch
Focuses on layout and spacing only - does not address color, typography, or content strategy
Relies on user's design tokens - assumes access to framework scales or ability to create custom properties
Cannot fix layout issues caused by framework constraints without refactoring component structure

use_cases.title: Frontend Developer Fixing Inconsistent Spacing
use_cases.description: A developer notices their dashboard uses random padding values (13px, 27px, 41px) throughout. They invoke the skill to establish a rem-based spacing scale and replace arbitrary values with systematic tokens.
use_cases.target_user: Frontend developers working on existing UIs

use_cases.title: Designer Breaking Card Grid Monotony
use_cases.description: A product designer sees their marketing page using identical icon-heading-text cards repeated six times. They use the skill to vary card sizes, introduce asymmetric layouts, and mix non-card content sections.

use_cases.title: Team Improving Visual Hierarchy
use_cases.description: A startup team has a feature landing page where no element stands out. The squint test reveals no clear primary action. They invoke the skill to apply generous whitespace, varied spacing, and strategic weight to guide user attention.
use_cases.target_user: Small teams building marketing or dashboard pages

prompt_templates.title: Fix Spacing Inconsistency
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my current spacing values and establish a consistent scale. The layout feels arbitrary and I want systematic spacing.
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when you notice random padding/margin values throughout your CSS or framework classes.

prompt_templates.title: Break Grid Monotony
prompt_templates.prompt: My page uses identical card grids everywhere. Help me create visual variety while maintaining cohesion.
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when every section follows the same icon-heading-text card pattern and looks repetitive.

prompt_templates.title: Strengthen Visual Hierarchy
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply the squint test to my layout. The most important elements aren't standing out.
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when users struggle to identify primary actions or content on first glance.

prompt_templates.title: Optimize Layout Tool Selection
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my use of CSS Grid vs Flexbox. Am I using the right tool for each layout pattern?
prompt_templates.scenario: Use when you want to ensure you're using the simplest appropriate layout method.

output_examples.input: My dashboard has random spacing values like padding: 13px, 27px, 41px scattered throughout.
output: Create CSS custom properties for semantic tokens: --space-section-inner, --space-card-padding

output_examples.input: Every section of my landing page uses the same 3-column card grid. It looks monotonous.
output: Vary section structure: 2-column feature spotlight, 4-column compact grid, full-width hero
Mix content types: testimonial quote, feature comparison table, step-by-step guide
Apply asymmetric layouts: offset image with text, staggered card sizes with 2-column spans

best_practices: Invoke /impeccable before using this skill to establish design context and anti-patterns awareness
Apply the squint test to verify hierarchy - blur your vision to identify primary, secondary, and grouped elements
Use gap for sibling spacing instead of margins to avoid margin collapse complications
Choose Flexbox for 1D layouts (nav bars, button groups) and Grid for 2D layouts (dashboards, page structure)
Apply clamp() for fluid spacing that scales gracefully across viewport sizes
anti_patterns: Using arbitrary spacing values outside a defined scale - randomness destroys visual cohesion
Making all spacing equal - variety in tight vs generous spacing creates rhythm and hierarchy
Nesting cards inside cards - use spacing and dividers for internal hierarchy instead
Defaulting to hero metric layouts with fake statistics - display actual user data or avoid the pattern
Centering every element - left-aligned content with asymmetric compositions feels more designed
Using arbitrary z-index values (999, 9999) - build a semantic scale (dropdown → sticky → modal)

faq.question: When should I use Flexbox vs CSS Grid?
faq.answer: Use Flexbox for 1D layouts like nav bars, button groups, and card contents. Use Grid for 2D layouts like dashboards, page-level structure, and data-dense interfaces where rows and columns need coordinated control.

faq.question: How do I choose spacing scale values?
faq.answer: Use your framework's built-in scale (Tailwind, Bootstrap, etc.) or create a rem-based custom scale. The specific values matter less than consistency - define a set and stick to it.

faq.question: What is the squint test for visual hierarchy?
faq.answer: Blur your vision or squint at your design. If you can still identify the most important element, the second most important, and clear content groupings, your hierarchy is working.

faq.question: Why avoid identical card grids everywhere?
faq.answer: Repetitive card grids create monotonous, predictable layouts. Vary structure with different content types, asymmetric compositions, and mixed sizing to create visual interest and guide attention.

faq.question: Should I center-align or left-align my content?
faq.answer: Left-aligned content with asymmetric layouts typically feels more designed. Reserve centering for specific emphasis moments - centering everything creates weak visual hierarchy.

faq.question: How do I create visual rhythm with spacing?
faq.answer: Alternate tight and generous spacing. Group related elements closely (8-12px), separate distinct sections generously (48-96px), and vary spacing within sections to avoid monotony.
